# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. I need a movie that is genuinely traumatizing. No cheap jump scares, just pure psychological or emotional weight.

**Score:** 154
**Body:** **I’m looking for a cinematic experience that is genuinely heavy and, frankly, traumatizing. I don't mean 'scary' in a typical horror sense-I mean movies that are so emotionally or psychologically intense that they leave a lasting scar.**

I’ve reached a point where 'standard' thrillers or dramas just don't cut it anymore. I want something that feels like a punch to the gut. Whether it’s a masterpiece like *Come and See*, a soul-crushing drama like *Requiem for a Dream*, or something modern that just feels 'wrong' like *Hereditary* or *The House That Jack Built*.

What is that one movie you watched once, loved, but can never bring yourself to watch again? Looking forward to your most disturbing recommendations!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Mothman Prophecies

Oldboy

Grave of the Fireflies
  - Love Mothman prophecies! Such an eerie feeling the entirety of the film 
2. Incendies 
3. Dancer in the Dark 
4. Irreversible
5. Martyrs
6. * Martyrs(2008)
* Bring Her Back
* We Need to Talk About Kevin
* Salo, or 120 Days of Sodom
* An American Crime(2007) and The Girl Next Door(2007)
* Dancer in the Dark
* Green Room
* Irreversible
* Man Bites Dog
7. Kids
8. Perfect Blue 
9. We Need to Talk About Kevin

Kids

Henry: Portrait of a Serial Killer

Good Boy (Norwegian one from 2022)


## 2. Quintessential British films?

**Score:** 29
**Body:** I’m 22 years old and I consider myself to be an Anglophile. Sometimes I fantasied myself in the UK especially places like Manchester or London. I’m from NY so it’s probably not different than NYC. What is considered to be the essential British film? Growing up, I’ve watched all the Harry Potter films, Nanny McPhee films, and Bridget Jones Diaries films. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. snatch, hot fuzz, trainspotting, shaun of the dead, lock stock and two smoking barrels, football factory, four lions, monty python and the holy grail, dead mans shoes, love actually, this is england, kidulthood, attack the block
  - Four Lions is one of my husbands favourite movies🥲
2. Withnail and I
3. Lawrence of Arabia (1962)
4. Alfie 

A Clockwork Orange

Monty Python’s Holy Grail

Hope and Glory

Shaun of the Dead/Hot Fuzz

Withnail and I

Lovely and Amazing

The Third Man

Billy Elliott

Pride

Four Weddings and a Funeral

The Collector

The Ladykillers

Hard Day’s Night

Sense and Sensibility 
5. Chariots of Fire. I think it came out in 1982, but definitely early 80s. One of those films that every Brit alive at that time saw and loved and it's part of their national identity.
6. Needs more Quadrophenia
7. Kind Hearts and Coronets
8. East is East
9. Basically anything directed/written by Mike Leigh is quintessentially British. Early made for TV films like Nuts in May and Abigails Party. Later feature films Life is Sweet, Naked, Secrets and Lies, Vera Drake are all wonderful examples of Leighs best of British works.

## 3. "intelligence is hot" movies

**Score:** 23
**Body:** i'm studying for my exams rn and i want to watch something with a charming intelligent character so it doesn't feel like a complete waste of time. i really enjoyed "legally blonde" but i'd also appreciate a more serious movie. thanks!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Real Genius 
2. Erin Brockovich (2000)
3. My Cousin Vinnie. 

A young lawyer and his fiance take on a murder case, despite a lack of trial experience. The early, fish out of water incompetent behavior disguises their intelligence. 

One of the better legal drama movies.
4. Check out Hidden Figures and The Shawshank Redemption.
5. Good Will Hunting

Set in Boston, the film follows Will Hunting (Damon), a troubled but self-taught mathematical genius working as a janitor at MIT, whose talent is discovered by professor Gerald Lambeau (Skarsgård). To avoid jail, Will agrees to study under Lambeau while attending therapy with psychologist Sean Maguire (Williams), which forces him to confront his past and his relationships, including with Harvard student Skylar (Driver).

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Good_Will_Hunting)
6. The Martian.    
Contact.
7. Moneyball is my personal favorite. Also love Michael Clayton. The Martian for a more scifi twist to it
8. I mean….Project Hail Mary, come on come on 
  - Rocky’s pretty hot, I guess.
    - Fist my bump

## 4. In need of movies to watch where someone slowly loses their mind

**Score:** 26
**Body:** What are some of the best films about someone who slowly loses their mind?   
I’m currently writing a film and need some inspiration. In the vein of fight club, black swan, and memento. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. In The Mouth of Madness 
2. The Machinist with Christian Bale could be a good watch. 
3. Shutter Island 

A Beautiful Mind 

Gone Girl 

The Shining 
4. Jacob’s Ladder
5. Moon
6. Angel Heart
7. Possession (1981)  
Backrooms (2026)  
Aniara (2018)  
Voyagers (2021)
8. Bug is my go to suggestion for this
9. A Scanner Darkly 
10. Videodrome

## 5. What's a movie that will lift me up on my birthday?

**Score:** 22
**Body:** Hello,

Today is my 34th birthday and it's been absolute shit ahaha. What's an inspiring, uplifting movie I can watch to raise my spirit? I usually would go for Disney moves or something super "gentle." But I've seen all those a million times and want an adult movie. I love mysteries (and usually watch horror/thriller/fantasy/true crime). But I just dont want something too serious or too heavy. I wanna just feel at ease and happy (but engaged!)

My sweet son is down for bed and I am hoping to relax and feel better 😌 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Maybe one of the Knives Out trilogy? They’re mysteries with an element of fun. Granted, there’s usually a murder, but it’s not too dark or depressing, just a kick off to the murder mystery aspect of the rest of the film. 
2. My Cousin Vinny (1992)
3. Stranger Than Fiction
4. I love Last Holiday. It’s a sweet feel good movie!
5. Secondhand Lions!
6. Peanut butter Falcon
7. The Blues Brothers 
8. I first saw The Big Lebowski on my birthday, so I will always think of it as a birthday movie
9. The Secret of Roan Inish. 
10. Secret Life of Walter Mitty

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Finally watched The Piano Teacher

**Score:** 37
**Body:** This movie kept me up all night. I should have known this would happen because when have I ever slept peacefully after a Haneke film?

I don't have more new thoughts to add to this film that haven't already surfaced in this subreddit and elsewhere except that it feels meta that this film lives on in cultural conversations as an "erotic film" or "that BDSM film". Heavy on the rot in erotic, I say. I've even seen Secretary (2002) recommended as a companion film. Personally I'd recommend Elle (2016) or Blue Velvet (1986). As I saw it, the film ends in a rape which is the final nail in the coffin of Erika's safely guarded inner world of desires, shame, control. Sure, initially the film lulls you into thinking this might be an erotic film, but it ends in psychosexual trauma. Categorically, or so I thought.   
  
This cultural flattening of Erika's trauma and complexity into "that BDSM movie" reflects how I imagine Walter went on with his life too. Blithely, unaware of the damage he caused; perhaps this affair comes up between chuckles on a boozy night with friends about that weird scene he once had with his uptight piano teacher. I didn't see him as a character to have enough reflection to construe his actions as rape because every time he faces something uncomfortable be it his idea of Erika versus Erika herself, or the puking incident, he responds to it with rage and domination. 

Is this memory of the film as a dark erotic film a reflection of something about us? About how we treat victims of sexual assault particularly those who do not come across as sympathetic? From the conversations about the film I scanned, admittedly during my sleepless feverish night, it disturbed me to see not everyone concludes Walter's actions are rape. He even taunts her with "is this what you wanted?", in what I read as an admission of his awareness that this is not what she wanted. He knows and she knows. It feels that much like Walter who dismissed this woman with the weird sexual desires, culturally too the film lives on in memory as that weird kinky transgressive film and not a despairingly traumatic one. 

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Is The Piano Teacher really labelled as an 'erotic' film by anyone??! I've never heard that. I've only ever heard the film referred to as deeply traumatic. But if you're right, that's just insane, and I'm guessing more to do with cynical marketing than any societal normalisation of rape. Hope I'm right about that anyway. 

It is a rape scene, yes, but it's greatly complicated by the fact that Erika has explicitly asked Walter to play the role of a dominating sexual sadist. She actually gives him a letter detailing explicit BDSM practices (including bondage and suffocation) she'd like him to play out with her. Without this request, I really doubt Walter would have behaved that way. 

What he gets catastrophically wrong is in imagining Erika wants him to abuse her in such a spontaneous and chaotic manner - and in front of her Mother! Walter believes he has her consent for any kind of degradation, and that it will be welcome. But he doesn't, not for that. 

For me the interesting question is why Erika chooses Walter to fulfil this desire. He's younger than her, a basic normie, and has zero experience with BDSM, nor, as far as we know, any desires in that direction. There's really nothing about him that would suggest he could play this role. 

My suspicion is that on some level Erika knows this, and her relationship with Walter is just another form of self harm. That's not to say that on any level she desires the rape - not at all. I don't think she anticipates that, and the tragedy is that when it happens her suffering is that of any woman. Her interest in BDSM in no way changes her experience of the rape. But I genuinely don't believe Walter understands any of this. I don't agree that Walter knows this isn't what Erika wants.
  - Yes, I distinctly remember The Piano Teacher being marketed as an erotic romance with dark undertones, in the United States at least. Check out the American trailer: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mjROGqRTMzQ

Funny Games had already put Haneke on the map for arthouse/foreign film buffs, so I knew he had a taste for the highly disturbing but really wasn't prepared for how perverted and depressing The Piano Teacher would be going into it. It made quite an impression, haven't forgotten it since and I've only been able to rewatch it once. I agree with you about Walter not getting it. He just wanted a normal romantic relationship with his hot talented milf teacher and was clueless about the intricacies of her particular brand of sexual mental illness. Not only was she a victim of her own psychology in the end, she brought him down with her, as if her disease was contagious. Walter is the most dynamic character in the film going from healthy, well-adjusted young man to a violent criminal because he wanted love and passion in his life from a woman he idealized at a distance without any clue of what he was getting into.
    - That trailer is hilarious. I wonder how many people it lured into the theatre, and I wonder how many of them walked out in shock!
  - Just want to say that yes imdb has it tagged as erotic. 

I've had it in my list of films to watch but not with friends for a year, for this reason lol
  - My impression is that it's not universally seen as a traumatic film and that the letter, Erika's desires are how a lot of the social media chatter frames this film. I went in to this film thinking it's some cheeky naughty film mostly coz of the joke in Sentimental Value and other media I've come across where seeing this movie is some kind of rite of giggly hush hush passage. Note to self - improve the media I consume.

I hadn't considered the question of why Erika chose Walter?  She is so nasty and unreachable, just tightly wound bundle of casual cruelty. Every scene with the other professors she sits apart from them, even in her field she doesn't fit in with anyone. So when a handsome, rich, young man shows eager interest in her she falls for it or confuses it with finally being safe enough to be seen. Her box of paraphernalia. suggested to me that she's done this before but that's not the only conclusion to draw from it. Perhaps she's just been waiting? The conversation he and she have at the recital in the beginning of the film, when she talks about the book about Schubert, about that fraction of a second before he loses his mind and is aware that this is what will happen to him is rather telling. That's the moment he happens to find her in.

With regards to the rape - Erika says 'this is what I want' and Walter responds 'fuck you, I'll show what you want'. So yes technically he plays out a version of her script but he also seems aware that he is consciously perverting her want and punishing her for it. For not factoring his wants. He's disgusted by her and he's disgusted by her puking earlier in the evening - how dare this pervert reject him? What he thought he signed up for was a typical cat-and-mouse conquest what he got was this complicated weirdo who made him feel small and emasculated. To those feelings he responded with violence. The reason why I also think Walter is innately violent or doesn't care about her boundaries is because of the way he follows her in the bathroom and jumps over the bathroom stall frame. Someone desperate for love or to be seen may take that as romance, I found his behavior predatory and unsettling.
    - > Her box of paraphernalia. suggested to me that she's done this before but that's not the only conclusion to draw from it. Perhaps she's just been waiting? 

I've never considered that she may have used the box of toys with other men in the past. I guess I'd always thought she was waiting. I've read the book on which the film is based, and maybe I have a vague recollection that there was a previous relationship. 

>the way he follows her in the bathroom and jumps over the bathroom stall frame. 

I had actually forgotten he does this! Perhaps you have a point. The film is very faithful to the book, but one thing that felt different to me in the book was the character of Walter. He's more obviously dislikable in the book, more of a chad. Perhaps it's simply the casting of Magimel in that role that alters it slightly. He's a likeable screen presence.
  - I also didn't know it was a Haneke film until it started. I just knew of it as a taboo film. So when the starting credits showed his name, I was scared for what I had unleashed into my living room on a Tuesday night. My other plan was to watch endless episodes of Come Dine With Me, a UK reality cooking show.  
2. I had heard that this movie would be very intense and it was. I was incredibly impressed with everyone's performances and I had a lot of intense takeaways from it. I was surprised to see Huppert in The Bedroom Window later, a Steve Guttenberg crime drama from the 80s. I was like "Is this is the SAME Isabelle Huppert? Wild." I'd recommend something like Black Swan as a double bill. I'm with you in that I didn't find The Piano Teacher to be an 'erotic thriller' or just a BDSM film or whatever. It was layered, courageous, and more complex than that.

## 2. Am I old or have the movies (at the theater) changed?

**Score:** 7
**Body:** So I am not saying "Movies today are at large, worse than the movies of ten years ago or longer." What I am saying is it seems like there is a certain kind of movie you don't see often at the theaters anymore.

On a lark I put on 'As good as it gets' with Jack Nicholson because it was in my vhs collection. And it's not the greatest thing ever but I enjoyed it. And then it got me thinking about some other older movies that I've seen lately: About Schmidt (which I think is actually great for what it is).

And other than Jack Nicholson, one of the things that I really enjoyed about these movies was that there were both just kinda... *chill*.

\- They're original IPs. The idea of a sequel for them isn't even something you'd consider, and they are not a part of any franchise.

\- The characters are very ordinary, and the problems that they are dealing with are very ordinary. No world ending threat, nothing even physically dangerous. The problems were very small, like "Will this ordinary person deal with this ordinary problem and make  their life better? The consequences of failure in these movies would just mean the main character's life doesn't become as good as it can be.

I almost felt challenged by these. It's like the movie was going "Hey idiot. Can you be bothered enough to care about this pudgy aging man with OCD get a girlfriend, without a CGI battle in the third act?"

And it felt good. It felt like I've been inside a rave for the past 4 years, I'm overstimulated, and I'm in a very calm spa and it feels indescribably calming.

 And I don't think I've seen a movie like this on the big screen in quite some time. Don't get me wrong, these movies do exist. The Holdovers came out a few  years ago and it was a phenomenal example of the kind of movie I'm talking about, and it might even be a new favourite of mine. But I don't know if it came out on the big screen.

Because if I'm correct then I think it would be sad if audiences didn't have the option to go see these kind of films on the big screen.



### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don't know if you have access to seeing movies in a sort of arthouse cinema but I'm finding that movies from all over the world are much more interesting than a lot of what is coming out of Hollywood. Drive My Car, Perfect Days, Sentimental Value, Parasite, Do Not Expect Too Much from the End of the World, Bacurau, The Secret Agent, Decision to Leave, Christian Petzold's movies, Woman at War.... so much good stuff in the last 10 years at least. I can see these in the theater but they aren't around long so I rely on Mubi and the Criterion Channel.
2. The best option these days is to look for films from smaller distributors like A24 and Neon. If you go back and watch Jaws, the granddaddy of summer blockbusters, these days it feels like an art-house film with three or more well developed characters, a compelling concept, a great location, and plenty pf background characters and settings completely filled in. Not to mention the excellent direction and cinematography.

Your intuition is correct. 
  - I’d also suggest foreign films. I’ve seen some great British or Irish films. 

The comic book franchises Hollywood churns out are simply boring unimaginative money grabs.
  - You know what? You're right. 

I JUST rewatched Jaws a couple weeks ago, and I almost didn't include it because it's a summer blockbuster where the main character fights a big monster, but it's actually quite low stakes when compared to the world ending threats we expect now (Not that those weren't around back in the day, ie; Terminator).

But yeah, a big part of the film is about Brody's fear of the water and his attempts to 'fit in.' Both on the island community and with the more competent and self-assured Cooper and Quint.
    - A movie from last year that pleasantly surprised me was Ballad of Wallis Island. It's very low-stakes beyond being about relationship difficulties between people. 

Hollywood films have always been commercialized. There is probably more of an emphasis on remakes and brand adaptations than in the past, but it's always been commercialized (Michael Bay's films in the 2000s and the endless Friday the 13th sequels in the 80s come to mind). You're probably just tired of mainstream films. 
3. I don't want to romanticize older movies too much but I do feel like even mediocre Hollywood movies from past decades had characters who felt more like human beings. Even the were actiony super cops had cramped desks and bad habits and stuff that felt uniquely theirs. The dialogue was more realistic and less functional. The characters feel like they live in the real world, not an extended break from it. Take a movie like Falling Down. Pretty good, but to me it's nothing special. Even so, in addition to the lead, Robert Duvall and all of Michael Douglas's victims and all the supporting cops all feel real and fleshed out in a way even the main character doesn't nowadays. 

I've also noticed movies have gotten a lot "emptier. Usually the only characters in a scene are characters who talk and have a role in the plot. If characters are meeting in a restaurant the other tables are almost all empty. If they're standing in a hall no one else is. If they're on the sidewalk it's only them. There's been a big uptick in closeups during these scenes to avoid people noticing, but once you do it'll drive you crazy. Guess streaming algorithms have decided extras are too expensive.

## 3. Films that reveal the 'true' protagonist

**Score:** 1
**Body:** Please share a film you enjoy that reveals the true protagonist being portayed initially as a secondary character actor/co-star throughout the story. 

My pick would be the 2008 Western **'Appaloosa'** *(it's currently free to watch on Tubi).* 

Throughout the movie, Ed Harris's character "Virgil Cole" is definitely perceived as the lead protagonist throughout the film: although ultimately the story arc and actions of his deputy and best friend "Everett Hitch" played by Viggo Mortensen: proves that in the end, HE was the main character/protagonist. The story is also narrated and told from Everett's perspective.

Additional comments/spolier:

>!We, the audience, are mesmerized by Virgil Cole the same way the townsfolk and Everett are. Virgil has the code, the quick draw, the authority, and the top billing. He feels like the center of gravity. But narrative structure doesn't care about star power or who has the loudest gun; it cares about agency and change. By the end of Appaloosa, Virgil is structurally paralyzed by his relationship with Allie. He’s willing to live a compromised life under the shadow of Bragg's freedom just to keep her. It is Everett who has to step outside the law, make the ultimate proactive sacrifice, and pull the trigger to restore actual justice and protect Virgil's honor. The fact that he rides off into the sunset alone: the ultimate lone cowboy archetype ending; seals it. He didn't just narrate the story; he owned the moral climax!<

There are several films that reveal a secondary character as the true protagonist, I look forward to discussing others and seeing what everyone comes up with.

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The musical Gypsy, which has two good film adaptations (although I personally like the one with Bette Midler the most), pulls this on the audience. The first half of the story mostly centers on child performer June, who is celebrated for her cuteness. Her more tomboyish co-performer Rose Lee is often sidelined. However, around halfway through, we see Rose Lee starting to find her independence and agency. >!And then the plot focuses on Rose Lee's career as a famous stripper.!<
  - Appreciate the contribution. I haven't seen this one but will put it on my list to watch! 

## 4. Backrooms Review

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Backrooms, directed by Kane Parsons, is a thriller-horror sci-fi film that centers on Chiwetel Ejiofor as Clark of Cap’n Clark’s Ottoman Empire, a run-down, second-rate “rip off” furniture store with hardly any traction or publicity that really has seen better days. The furniture store isn’t the only thing that’s run down. Clark is not a happy-go-lucky guy either, despite what his commercials might suggest. Clark, wearing a pirate suit, does not save him from being a divorced, down-on-his-luck alcoholic with no keys to his house, which he pays for as he does with everything else. Clark hates his life; he's unhappy and wants to be more than just a “furniture store owner”, a very relatable issue that I’m sure many in retail can understand and relate to.

The store Cap’n Clark’s Ottoman Empire has also been acting strange lately, with lights flickering on and off, an odd, peculiar circuit breaker that nobody knows where it’s actually connected to, as well as an inter-dimensional wall that can best be described as a maze, hence the name “Backrooms.” These Backrooms are literally a never-ending maze; each one is designed differently, with many objects and belongings left by people before Clark and his store existed. Determined to prove that what Clark is actually seeing is reality, he gets two of his employees to help him record what’s behind the Backrooms, and what transpires is a surreal cluster fuck of an experience. The best way to summarize this film is “We Live In A Simulation Man.” It might be an ignorant way to describe it, but it literally is. Thank God I didn’t take any edibles before seeing this, or man would I have had a hell of a fucking ride!

I went into this completely blind, having no prior knowledge about it (like I did with Obsession), or that it was based on a horror YouTube Series or that it was a Creepypasta. I might have at one point heard about it, but I never really went down a rabbit hole exploring it before the film’s release, which is why my experience definitely was different from other people who were familiar with the actual lore of Backrooms. Is this film for everybody? No. It’s definitely a film you should see with friends or a decent crowd to experience not just the scares but actually what in the living hell is going on as you’re watching it, trying to put the pieces together. It’s not a typical horror film that most are used to and comfortable with, that is absolutely for sure, and I’m sure something like this in the horror-thriller sci-fi genre has been done in the past.

I did like Backrooms regardless of my lack of knowledge or interest in the actual lore of the story. I loved the 1990’s mise en scene from the clothing, tech, and the overall aesthetic, especially the use of the VHS-style camcorder. The grainy, choppy recording in the beginning was a nice touch, adding that artificial discovery of something weird and begotten that’s not within our logic. It is scary, but it’s not straight-up “jump scares.” It takes its time reeling spectators into the Backrooms and what’s in store for them. No spoilers, but I did love the tribute they did to a specific horror film from the 1970’s (People who have seen it know what I’m talking about).

Chiwetel Ejiofor, as far as his character Clark did a great job, but so did his shrink, played by Renate Reinsve as Dr. Mary Kline, as well as her own story of trauma and how she and Clark aren’t exactly so different from each other. Both of these characters had great chemistry on screen, especially during their session and later on in the film. The two Film School Boyfriend and Girlfriend couple, Bobby and Kat, were good too in their parts. There are other characters, obviously, but that goes into other parts of the film that I don’t want to give away.

Overall, Backrooms is a good new horror to check out during the Summer Blockbuster season. Even if you’re not super invested or a fan of the lore, give it a shot. Make this and Obsession a double feature and support something new from upcoming filmmakers coming into the horror genre. We definitely need that endorsement these days. Sorry, I’m late to the party again. Feel free to roast me or send me any lore that is recommended for me to look into on Backrooms.  
I give this film a B.

## 5. Would a non-horror fan like Obsession?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I've always found it hard to suspend my disbelief while watching horror movies for some reason. I hope this doesn't offend anyone, but most of the ones that I've watched like Halloween and Insidious just seemed silly, not scary. The only horror movie I've watched that I found a bit disturbing was The Substance, but that was more disgust than terror. It's strange because psychological thrillers are probably my favorite genre, but I get bored by any horror movies that involve monsters, ghosts, demons, or any of that stereotypical stuff.

I don't know anything about the plot of Obsession, but it's been impossible not to hear all the hype around it. I want to watch a movie this weekend, and my choices are down to this or Supergirl. Do you think I'd be able to enjoy Obsession?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I personally didn't think it was the greatest movie ever made, but my main issue was that the trailer was very spoiler heavy and gave away almost all the jump scares. 

The movie is paced very different from the usual horror experience. It plays up more of this indie drama about a very socially awkward guy who gets to make a wish. Like there isn't a sense of persistent dread, such as a killer lurking in the house or some malevolent entity in the shadows. Because of that, the movie has to do a lot sudden loud noises and jump scares to meet the horror quota. I'd say for the most part, it's pretty accessible given how much it tries to avoid more traditional genre tropes without entirety forgetting what genre it exists in. The main thing is it's this toxic love story where a wish goes as badly as can be.

5. I thought it was a 2/10 film. Not scary, unintentionally funny, yet annoying when it was trying to be funny, with quite irritating dialogue, whose delivery that felt like an unrehearsed YouTube sketch more than a serious film. The main male character’s acting is absolutely flat, and the lack of direction is so palpable it’s all I could see. I need to see him in something else to know who to point the finger at.

This film was high noobery, I was sitting in the theater thinking I had been pranked, or that the internet is now a matrix level false reality consisting entirely of marketing bots. This movie’s reception is a mass hallucination, that I had the uncomfortable pleasure of learning I have an absolute immunity to.

I’m dead serious, it was 2 hours of existential confusion. It was like when you go to YouTube on a private browser, see the trending page, and think “dear god this is what people like to watch?” This movie was so bad it held a mirror up to my previously unknown pretentiousness, then my own fist came out of it to hit me.

The themes are the high point, so for that I gave them 1 point. Another point for the main actress’s performance, it was the only source of entertainment, and she did a great job. On the other hand, her performance highlights how lifeless every other performer was.

So in conclusion, DO NOT see this movie because of hype. See it because the plot summary sounds like something you would find entertaining, even though that is not the right word for a film with these themes.
